title: The Lattice of Integer Flows of a Regular Matroid
overall_summary: The paper studies the integer flow lattice Λ(M)=ker(A)∩Z^E of a regular matroid M represented by a totally unimodular matrix A, with the Euclidean inner product inherited from Z^E. Its main theorem says that Λ(M) determines M up to contraction of co-loops: Λ(M) and Λ(N) are isometric if and only if M• and N• are isomorphic. The core technique is a metric characterization of signed circuits, called simple flows: a nonzero flow α is a signed circuit exactly when every nontrivial decomposition α=β+γ has inner product ⟨β,γ⟩<0. This characterization is proved using Tutte’s conformal or consistent decomposition of flows into signed circuits, and it is the part most directly useful for problems about irreducible lattice elements. A second major technique reconstructs the support pattern of a totally unimodular column matrix from its Gram matrix by inclusion-exclusion and then uses row and column sign changes to recover the matroid representation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3 and Lemma 4, basic unimodular signing tools
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For an integer matrix, totally unimodular means that every square submatrix has determinant in {−1,0,+1}, and weakly unimodular means that every maximal square submatrix has determinant in {−1,0,+1}. Lemma 3 states: if an m by s weakly unimodular matrix U contains the s by s identity matrix I_s as a submatrix, then U is totally unimodular. Lemma 4, attributed to Camion, states: if Q and U are totally unimodular matrices with the same entrywise absolute value matrix, Q♯=U♯, then Q can be changed into U by multiplying some rows and some columns by −1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 3, the paper says the proof is elementary and omits it. The standard argument is to take any k by k minor using a row set R and column set C. If the minor contains an identity row corresponding to a column outside C, then that row is zero in the selected columns and the determinant is 0. Otherwise add, for each column outside C, the corresponding identity row to extend the minor to an s by s maximal minor. After ordering rows and columns, the extended determinant is ± the original determinant, so weak unimodularity forces the original determinant to lie in {−1,0,+1}. Lemma 4 is used as a black box from Camion: two totally unimodular signings of the same {0,1}-matrix differ only by independent row and column sign switches.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5, coordinate matrices of flow-lattice bases are weakly unimodular
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (M,E) be a regular matroid of rank r on a ground set E of size m, let s=m−r, and let Λ(M)=ker(A)∩Z^E be its integer flow lattice for a totally unimodular representation A. If B is any Z-basis of Λ(M), and Q is the E by s matrix whose columns are the elements of B in the chosen coordinates, then Q is weakly unimodular.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a base B0 of the matroid and coordinatize a totally unimodular representation by B0, so the representing matrix has the form [I_r L]. The associated fundamental flow basis is given by the columns of U=[−L; I_s], and U is totally unimodular. Since both the columns of U and the columns of Q are Z-bases of the same lattice Λ(M), there is an integral change-of-basis matrix F with determinant ±1 such that Q=UF. Every maximal square minor of Q is the determinant of a corresponding maximal square minor of U multiplied by det(F), after selecting rows; because U is totally unimodular and det(F)=±1, these determinants are in {−1,0,+1}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 6, 7, and 8, circuit supports and signed circuits
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a regular matroid represented by a totally unimodular matrix A over a ground set E, and let Λ(M)=ker(A)∩Z^E. For β∈Λ(M), let supp(β)={e∈E:β(e)≠0}. Lemma 6 states: if β∈Λ(M) and supp(β) is a circuit C of M, then β spans the real vector subspace of ker(A) consisting of all vectors supported inside C. Lemma 7 states: under the same hypothesis, all nonzero coordinates of β have the same absolute value. A simple flow or signed circuit is a nonzero element of Λ(M) whose coordinates lie in {−1,0,+1} and whose support is a circuit of M. Lemma 8 states: for every circuit C of M there are exactly two simple flows with support C, namely ±α_C.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 6, if the kernel restricted to the columns in C had dimension at least 2, then a nontrivial linear combination of two independent kernel vectors could be chosen to cancel one coordinate while remaining nonzero. Its support would be a proper nonempty subset of C that is dependent, contradicting minimal dependence of the circuit C. For Lemma 7, write one column of the submatrix A_C as a linear combination of the others and reduce to an irredundant linear system. Cramer’s rule expresses the coefficients as ratios of maximal minors of a totally unimodular matrix, so all nonzero coefficients have absolute value 1 relative to a common scale. Lemma 8 follows by taking any nonzero kernel vector supported on the dependent set C; minimality gives support exactly C, Lemmas 6 and 7 show the kernel on C is one-dimensional with equal absolute coordinate values, and normalization gives precisely the two {−1,0,+1}-valued choices ±α_C.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 9 and 10, fundamental bases consist of simple flows and simple flows are representation-invariant
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a regular matroid, let B be a base of M, and coordinatize a totally unimodular representation by B so that the representing matrix is [I_r L], where r is the rank and s=|E|−r. The associated fundamental basis B(M,B) of Λ(M) is the set of columns of U=[−L; I_s]. Lemma 9 states that every element of B(M,B) is a simple flow, and hence the set S(M) of all simple flows spans Λ(M). Lemma 10 states that if A and A′ are two matrices representing the same regular matroid M, then, under the signed-permutation coordinate identification coming from uniqueness of regular matroid representations, the simple flows relative to A are exactly the simple flows relative to A′.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 9, the j-th column of U records the unique linear dependence obtained by adjoining the j-th non-base element to the base B. Its support is the fundamental circuit contained in B∪{e_j}, and the entries are in {−1,0,+1} because the representation is totally unimodular; hence each column is a signed circuit. Since these columns form a Z-basis of Λ(M), simple flows span the lattice. Lemma 10 uses uniqueness of representations of regular matroids: two real representations differ by an invertible row operation, a signed permutation of columns, and a field automorphism, which over R is trivial. The signed column permutation preserves circuits, supports up to relabeling, and the property that all nonzero coordinates are ±1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 11, Tutte consistent decomposition of flows
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (M,E) be a regular matroid represented by a weakly unimodular matrix A, and let Λ(M)=ker(A)∩Z^E. A consistent decomposition of a flow β∈Λ(M) is a multiset 𝒜 of simple flows such that β=Σ_{α∈𝒜}α, supp(α)⊆supp(β) for every α∈𝒜, and α(e)β(e)≥0 for every α∈𝒜 and every e∈E. Proposition 11 states that every β∈Λ(M) has a consistent decomposition.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof first shows that every nonzero flow β has a simple flow α conforming to it, meaning supp(α)⊆supp(β) and α(e)β(e)>0 for all e∈supp(α). Suppose not, and choose a counterexample β with supp(β) minimal. Its support is not a circuit, otherwise Lemmas 6–8 would provide the required signed circuit. Since supp(β) is dependent, it contains a circuit, hence contains the support of some simple flow α. Choose e∈supp(α) for which |β(e)| is minimal, and choose the sign of α so that α(e) has the same sign as β(e). Subtract the integer multiple of α that cancels the e-coordinate, namely (β(e)/α(e))α since α(e)=±1. The resulting flow β′ has strictly smaller support. If β′=0, then α conforms to β; otherwise minimality gives a simple flow α′ conforming to β′. The minimal choice of |β(e)| ensures that subtracting this multiple did not reverse signs on any remaining coordinate, so α′ also conforms to β, a contradiction. Having found a conforming simple flow α for every nonzero β, the proposition follows by induction on ||β||_1=Σ_e |β(e)|. For β=0 the empty multiset works. For β≠0, choose conforming α; then β−α has smaller l1-norm and still has support and signs compatible with β, so a consistent decomposition of β−α together with α gives one for β.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 12, metric characterization of simple flows
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (M,E) be a regular matroid represented by a weakly unimodular matrix A, let Λ(M)=ker(A)∩Z^E with the Euclidean inner product ⟨·,·⟩, and let α be a nonzero element of Λ(M). Then the following are equivalent: (a) α is a simple flow, i.e. a signed circuit; (b) for every pair of nonzero β,γ∈Λ(M) such that α=β+γ, one has ⟨β,γ⟩<0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume first that α is a simple flow. For each coordinate e, α(e) is 0, +1, or −1, and α(e)=β(e)+γ(e) with β(e),γ(e) integers. This forces β(e)γ(e)≤0 for every e. If supp(β) and supp(γ) were disjoint, then both supports would be nonempty subsets of supp(α), and each nonzero flow support contains a circuit; this would put a proper circuit inside the circuit supp(α), impossible. Hence supp(β) and supp(γ) overlap, so at some coordinate β(e)γ(e)<0. Summing over coordinates gives ⟨β,γ⟩<0. Conversely, suppose α is not a simple flow. By Proposition 11, α has a consistent decomposition into simple flows. Since α≠0, the decomposition is nonempty. If it had one term, α itself would be simple, contrary to assumption, so it has at least two terms. Choose one term β and put γ=α−β, which is nonzero. Consistency gives β(e)γ(e)≥0 for every coordinate e, so ⟨β,γ⟩≥0. Thus condition (b) fails.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 13, isometries preserve simple elements
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For an arbitrary integral lattice Λ, define S(Λ) to be the set of nonzero elements α∈Λ such that for all nonzero β,γ∈Λ with α=β+γ one has β·γ<0. If ψ:Λ→Λ′ is an isometry of integral lattices, then ψ restricts to a metric-preserving bijection S(Λ)→S(Λ′).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The defining condition for membership in S(Λ) is expressed only in terms of the abelian group structure, decompositions α=β+γ, and the bilinear form. An isometry preserves addition, nonzero elements, and inner products, and its inverse has the same properties. Therefore α satisfies the defining condition in Λ if and only if ψ(α) satisfies it in Λ′.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 14 and Corollary 15, reconstructing support intersections from a Gram matrix
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let B={β_1,...,β_s} be a set of column vectors in {−1,0,+1}^E, let U be the E by s matrix with these columns, let A=(a_ij)=U†U be the Gram matrix, and let C_i=supp(β_i). For a collection C={C_1,...,C_s}, define φ_C(S)=|∩_{i∈S} C_i| for S⊆[s], with ∩∅=E, and γ_C(S)=|(∩_{i∈S} C_i) \ (∪_{j∈[s]\S} C_j)|. For a symmetric integer s by s matrix A with positive diagonal, classify a triple {h,i,j} as positive, null, or negative according as a_hi a_ij a_jh is positive, zero, or negative; let Δ(A) be the set of negative triples. Define f_A(∅)=0, define f_A(S)=0 if S contains a negative triple, define f_A({i})=a_ii, and otherwise define f_A(S)=min{|a_ij|:{i,j}⊆S}. Define g_A(S)=Σ_{S⊆S′⊆[s]} (−1)^{|S′\S|} f_A(S′). Proposition 14 states: if U is totally unimodular, then for every nonempty S⊆[s], f_A(S)=φ_C(S) and g_A(S)=γ_C(S); in particular A is g-nonnegative, meaning g_A(S)≥0 for every nonempty S. Corollary 15 states: if U is totally unimodular and A=U†U, then the rows of the {0,1}-matrix X(A), defined by putting exactly g_A(S) copies of the indicator row of S for each nonempty S⊆[s], can be permuted so that they are exactly the nonzero rows of U♯.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For singletons, f_A({i})=a_ii=⟨β_i,β_i⟩=|C_i|=φ_C({i}). For pairs {i,j}, take any two rows e,f in C_i∩C_j. The 2 by 2 submatrix on rows e,f and columns i,j has all entries ±1. Total unimodularity rules out determinant ±2, so the determinant is 0 and the two rows are proportional; equivalently β_i(e)β_j(e)=β_i(f)β_j(f). Thus the product β_i(e)β_j(e) is constant over C_i∩C_j, and |a_ij|=|C_i∩C_j|. For triples, if e lies in C_h∩C_i∩C_j, then the pairwise signs force a_hi a_ij a_jh>0; hence a negative or null triple cannot have a common intersection, and f_A gives 0 in that case. It remains to handle sets S of size at least 3 all of whose triples are positive. If f_A(S)≠φ_C(S), choose a minimal such S. By switching signs of columns one may make all a_ij positive on S, and by switching signs of rows one may make all β_i(e)=1 whenever i∈S and e∈C_i. Choose {i,j}⊆S with a_ij minimal, so f_A(S)=a_ij and φ_C(S)≤|C_i∩C_j|=a_ij. Minimality forces strict inequality and, for each h∈S, an element e_h lying in all C_l with l∈S\{h} but not in C_h. The submatrix on rows e_h and columns indexed by S is, up to permutations, J_t−I_t for t=|S|≥3. Its determinant is ±(t−1), not in {−1,0,+1}, contradicting total unimodularity. This proves f_A=φ_C on nonempty sets, and inclusion-exclusion gives g_A=γ_C. Corollary 15 follows because γ_C(S) counts exactly the number of rows of U whose support, as a subset of the column set [s], is precisely S.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 16, a Gram-equal weakly unimodular matrix is totally unimodular
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let U be an m by s totally unimodular matrix containing I_s as a submatrix. If Q is a weakly unimodular matrix, with s columns, such that Q†Q=U†U, then Q is totally unimodular.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on s. The case s=1 is immediate because weak unimodularity already forces all entries to be 0 or ±1. For the induction step, first show every nonsingular (s−1) by (s−1) submatrix Z′ of Q has determinant ±1. Extend Z′ to a nonsingular s by s submatrix Z of Q. Since Q is weakly unimodular, det(Z)=±1, so F=Z^{-1} is an integral matrix with det(F)=±1. Then QF is weakly unimodular and contains I_s as a submatrix, so Lemma 3 makes QF totally unimodular. After permuting rows, QF and UF can be interpreted as fundamental bases of integer flow lattices of regular matroids. Because Q†Q=U†U, the corresponding column bases have the same Gram matrix, hence define an isometry between the two lattices. The columns of QF are simple flows, so Lemma 13 and Proposition 12 imply the columns of UF are simple flows as well; therefore all entries of UF lie in {−1,0,+1}. Since U contains I_s, the matrix UF contains F as a submatrix, so every entry of F is 0 or ±1. But F=Z^{-1}=adj(Z)/det(Z), so the entries of F are the signed (s−1)-minors of Z; hence det(Z′)=±1. Now delete any column i from Q and U, obtaining Q_i and U_i. The preceding paragraph shows Q_i is weakly unimodular, while U_i is totally unimodular and contains I_{s−1}. Also Q_i†Q_i=U_i†U_i. By induction Q_i is totally unimodular. This holds for every deleted column, giving all minors of Q of size at most s−1; the s by s minors are controlled by weak unimodularity. Hence Q is totally unimodular.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1 and Corollary 2, reconstruction of regular matroids and 3-connected graphs from flow lattices
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a regular matroid M, let M• be the minor obtained by contracting all co-loops of M. Theorem 1 states: if M and N are regular matroids, then their integer flow lattices Λ(M) and Λ(N) are isometric if and only if M• and N• are isomorphic. Corollary 2 states: if G and H are 3-connected finite graphs, then Λ(G) and Λ(H), the lattices of integer flows of the graphs, are isometric if and only if G and H are isomorphic.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the forward direction of the easy implication, assume M•≅N•. Choose corresponding bases and coordinatize both co-loop-free minors by the same totally unimodular matrix [I_r L]. If M has p co-loops and N has q co-loops, then M and N can be represented by block matrices with leading identity blocks I_p and I_q for the co-loops and the same [I_r L] block for the rest. Their fundamental flow-basis matrices are [0_{p by s}; −L; I_s] and [0_{q by s}; −L; I_s], which have identical Gram matrices; hence the flow lattices are isometric. Conversely, let ψ:Λ(M)→Λ(N) be an isometry. Choose a fundamental basis β_1,...,β_s of Λ(M) and let U be its coordinate matrix, so U is totally unimodular and contains I_s. Let Q be the coordinate matrix of ψ(β_1),...,ψ(β_s) in a fixed representation of N. The Gram matrices agree, Q†Q=U†U, and Lemma 5 makes Q weakly unimodular. Lemma 16 then implies Q is totally unimodular. By Corollary 15, after permuting rows, the nonzero rows of U♯ and Q♯ are the same matrix X(A), where A=U†U=Q†Q; zero rows correspond to co-loops. Because X(A) contains an identity block, write the nonzero part as [K; I_s] in absolute value. Camion’s lemma allows row and column sign changes so that the nonzero parts of U and Q become identical, say [−L; I_s]. Thus M and N admit representations with the same non-co-loop block [I_r L], while their only difference is the number of co-loops. Therefore M•≅N•. Corollary 2 follows from Whitney’s theorem that 3-connected graphs are isomorphic exactly when their graphic matroids are isomorphic, together with the fact that a 3-connected graph has no cut-edges, so its graphic matroid has no co-loops.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollaries 17 and 18, cut-lattice versions by duality
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a regular matroid M represented by A, the lattice of integer cuts is Γ(M)=Row(A)∩Z^E with the induced Euclidean inner product. Let M◦ denote the minor obtained from M by deleting all loops. Corollary 17 states: for regular matroids M and N, Γ(M) and Γ(N) are isometric if and only if M◦ and N◦ are isomorphic. Corollary 18 states: for regular matroids M and N, Λ(M) and Γ(N) are isometric if and only if M• is isomorphic to (N◦)*, equivalently to (N*)•.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper observes that Λ(M) is isometric to Γ(M*) for regular matroids, because if M is represented in coordinates by [I_r L], then the dual M* is represented by [−L† I_s], and the corresponding flow and cut lattices match. Loops of M correspond to co-loops of M*, and contraction/deletion are interchanged by duality. Applying Theorem 1 to the dual matroids gives Corollary 17, and applying the same duality once to compare a flow lattice to a cut lattice gives Corollary 18.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 19, intrinsic characterization of lattices that are regular-matroid flow lattices
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a lattice basis B, call B g-nonnegative, g-positive, or g-feasible when its Gram matrix has that property. A symmetric integer matrix A with positive diagonal is g-feasible if there is a totally unimodular matrix U with U†U=A; it is g-positive if it is g-nonnegative and g_A({i})>0 for every singleton {i}. Proposition 19 states that for an integral lattice Λ, the following are equivalent: (a) Λ has a g-feasible basis; (b) Λ has a basis that is both g-feasible and g-positive; (c) Λ is isometric to Λ(M) for some regular matroid M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (a)⇒(b), choose a g-feasible basis with Gram matrix A, and choose a totally unimodular matrix U whose columns have Gram matrix A, equivalently a suitable signing of X(A) after deleting zero rows. Since A has full rank, U has an invertible s by s submatrix Z. Total unimodularity gives det(Z)=±1, so F=Z^{-1} is integral with determinant ±1. Then Q=UF is weakly unimodular and contains I_s, so Lemma 3 makes Q totally unimodular. The columns of Q are another basis of the same lattice, and because Q contains I_s, Proposition 14 implies its Gram matrix is g-positive as well as g-feasible. For (b)⇒(c), take a g-feasible g-positive Gram matrix A and a totally unimodular signing U of X(A) with U†U=A. Since g-positivity supplies unit rows, after row and column sign changes U can be arranged to contain I_s; writing U=[−L; I_s] constructs a regular matroid represented by [I_r L], whose fundamental flow basis has Gram matrix A. Hence Λ is isometric to Λ(M). The implication (c)⇒(b) follows by taking any fundamental basis of Λ(M): Lemma 9 gives a signed-circuit basis, and Proposition 14 shows its Gram matrix is g-feasible and g-positive.

proof_techniques.name: Metric indecomposability detects signed circuits
proof_techniques.description: The paper defines, for an arbitrary integral lattice, simple elements as nonzero elements α for which every decomposition α=β+γ into nonzero lattice elements has β·γ<0. In regular-matroid flow lattices, Proposition 12 proves that these metric simple elements are exactly the simple flows, i.e. signed circuits. The proof uses only the coordinate restrictions of a signed circuit and circuit minimality in one direction, and Tutte’s consistent decomposition in the other direction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The irreducibility condition in the original problem is exactly the negation of the existence of a decomposition with nonnegative cross term, so the paper’s simple-element criterion is directly analogous. A possible route is to identify some vertices with circuit-like minimal objects under an auxiliary representation, or to prove an analogue of Proposition 12 for the tree lattice.

proof_techniques.name: Conformal decomposition by minimal support
proof_techniques.description: Tutte’s decomposition starts by proving that every nonzero flow has a signed circuit contained in its support and with compatible coordinate signs. The key choice is a minimal-support counterexample, then a circuit inside its support, then a coordinate of minimal absolute value on that circuit; subtracting the largest compatible multiple reduces support without reversing signs. Iterating gives a decomposition of any flow into signed circuits that all lie inside the original support and point in the same coordinate directions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a lattice with a preferred graph basis, a similar minimal-support reduction could turn a hypothetical reducible vertex or vector into smaller graph-supported pieces. This may help convert algebraic decomposability into a combinatorial statement about connected supports or minimal subtrees.

proof_techniques.name: Coordinate sign obstruction for decompositions of {−1,0,+1}-vectors
proof_techniques.description: In the proof of Proposition 12, if a signed circuit α is written as α=β+γ, then each coordinate of α is 0 or ±1, so integer coordinates force β(e)γ(e)≤0 for every e. Circuit minimality then ensures β and γ overlap somewhere, making at least one coordinate product negative and hence the whole Euclidean inner product negative.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a vertex of the given tree lattice can be embedded or represented as a minimal {−1,0,+1}-supported vector in a positive definite coordinate lattice, this argument would prove its irreducibility. Even without such an embedding, the pattern suggests proving coordinatewise nonpositivity plus unavoidable overlap.

proof_techniques.name: Fundamental circuit bases from matroid bases
proof_techniques.description: Given a regular matroid representation [I_r L], the matrix [−L; I_s] has columns forming a Z-basis of the flow lattice. Each column is the signed circuit associated with adding one non-base element to the chosen base. This provides a basis all of whose elements are metrically simple, and the simple flows span the whole flow lattice.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the vertex basis of the tree lattice can be compared to a fundamental circuit or cocircuit basis, then irreducibility of some or all basis elements may follow from the paper’s signed-circuit argument. This is especially suggestive because the tree form has a graph-indexed Gram matrix.

proof_techniques.name: Recovering support data from the Gram matrix
proof_techniques.description: The paper reconstructs the nonzero row supports of a totally unimodular column matrix U from A=U†U. Pairwise intersections are recovered from |a_ij|, triple sign conditions determine when higher intersections vanish, and an inclusion-exclusion transform g_A counts rows with any prescribed support. Total unimodularity is used critically to rule out a J_t−I_t submatrix whose determinant would be ±(t−1).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original lattice is specified only by its Gram matrix in the vertex basis. If one tries to realize that Gram matrix inside a standard coordinate lattice or as a cut/flow lattice, the g_A support-reconstruction method gives concrete constraints on possible supports and may force the existence of a metric simple basis vector.

proof_techniques.name: Row and column sign normalization
proof_techniques.description: Camion’s lemma says that two totally unimodular signings of the same support matrix differ only by row and column sign switches. The paper combines this with Gram-matrix support reconstruction to upgrade equality of Gram matrices into equality of the underlying matroid representation, up to irrelevant zero rows.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When signs of off-diagonal pairings are fixed by a tree, sign-switching can be a useful normalization device. It may allow a decomposition or embedding problem to be reduced to a canonical all-negative-on-edges form.

proof_techniques.name: Weak-to-total unimodularity via an identity submatrix
proof_techniques.description: Several arguments exploit the fact that a weakly unimodular matrix containing an identity block is automatically totally unimodular. Lemma 16 further shows that if a weakly unimodular matrix has the same Gram matrix as a totally unimodular matrix containing an identity block, then it is totally unimodular; the proof uses induction, lattice isometries, and preservation of simple elements.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proposed coordinate matrix for the tree lattice has maximal minors controlled and contains a visible identity block, these lemmas suggest a way to obtain strong total-unimodularity conclusions. Such conclusions can convert metric information into graph or matroid structure.

proof_techniques.name: Flow-cut duality
proof_techniques.description: The paper notes that the lattice of integer flows of a regular matroid is isometric to the lattice of integer cuts of its dual. Consequently, all flow-lattice reconstruction results have cut-lattice versions, with co-loops replaced by loops. This is particularly natural for graphic matroids, where cut lattices are closely related to incidence matrices.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The Gram matrix of a graph cut basis has diagonal degree terms and negative adjacency terms, resembling the tree-indexed bilinear form in the original problem. The cut-lattice viewpoint may therefore be more directly adaptable than the flow-lattice viewpoint.

proof_techniques.name: Discarding zero rows and co-loops
proof_techniques.description: In the reconstruction theorem, zero rows of a flow-basis matrix correspond exactly to co-loops of the matroid: elements that occur in no flow and do not affect the flow lattice. The proof separates the common nonzero support matrix from arbitrary leading zero rows, showing that the lattice determines only the co-loop-free minor.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If some vertices or coordinates in an auxiliary model contribute no essential decomposability information, it may be useful to isolate and discard them, focusing on the part detected by the bilinear form.

other_useful_info: The paper cites Bacher, de la Harpe, and Nagnibeda, and Biggs, for foundational material on graph flow and cut lattices. It cites Tutte’s work on chain-groups for the conformal decomposition theorem used as Proposition 11. Whitney’s theorem on 2-isomorphism is used to derive the graph corollary for 3-connected graphs. The paper’s examples identify the root lattice A_n with Λ(U_{1,n+1}) and with Γ(U_{n,n+1}), and identify the body-centered cubic lattice with Λ(K_4) and Γ(K_4); these examples may be useful reference points for recognizing familiar positive definite lattices as regular-matroid flow or cut lattices.
why_relevant: Gives structural results for graphic and regular-matroid flow lattices, a major source of graph-lattice techniques used by Greene. Its circuit/flow viewpoint can help translate decomposability of lattice vectors into connectivity properties of graphs.
